content.full_text:
Re:
Farn Polrucer PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56@ o Fax (916) 322-0886
Novernber 17,2004
Dennis Zell
Janet Fogarty & Associates
1633 Bayshore Highway, Suite232
Burlingame, CA 94010
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-04-176
DearMr. Zell:
This letter responds to your request on behalf of your client Nano Maldonado, for
reconsideration of advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act')-' We provide informalassistance since the current election cycle is over and
Mr. Maldonado is enjoined from marketing his billboard to off-premises advertisers.
Until he secures relief from the courts or obtains an 'bffipremises" permit from Caltrans,
there is no basis for departing from our earlier advice.2
QUESTIONS
1. Do any laws or regulations enforced by the FPPC prohibit Mr. Maldonado from
leasing his billboard space for $10,000 per side per month to a candidate for
California Assembly?
2. Assuming the answer to question number I is 'ho," would Mr. Maldonado have
to report any amount as a contribution, or would the $10,000 per side per month
cost simply constitute an expenditure of the campaign?
3. For purposes of FPPC reporting, may the fair market value of a billboard that is
restricted to noncommercial advertising be established by the highest bid received
for the particular lease period, where the use of the billboard has been offered on
equal terms to all candidates in the district who have reported that they have
raised at least as much as the minimum bid for the billboard?
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not confei the immunity conferred by a Commission opinion or formal written
advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).) (Copy enclosed.)
,-*=


File No. I-04-176
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
In ourprior letter (Zell Advice Letter, No. 4-04-142)we declined to advise that
your client's willingness to accept the price offered in a candidate "auction" would
establish the fair market value for his billboard. In reviewing this advice, we conclude
that we cannot agee with your contention that we should answer your questions based on
an assumed fair market value of $10,000 per side per month.
FACTS
You are an attorney representing Nano Maldonado, the owner of a billboard
located along Highway l0l in Redwood City. Mr. Maldonado has been engaged in a
dispute with the Califomia Departrnent of Transportation ("Caltrans') going back to
1991, which is more fully described in a recent opinion published by the United States
Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit.r Mr. Maldonado's dispute with Caltrans grew out
of that agency's denial of his application for a permit to use his Redwood City billboard
for off-premises advertising. Calkans derried the application because the billboard is
located on a portion of Highway 101 classified as a "landscaped freewa5/'tmder the
California Outdoor Advertising Act (*COAA"), California Business & Professions Code
sections 52W5486. As pertinent herq COAA sections 5440 and 5442 prohibit billboard
advertisements along "landscalred freeways" unless the advertisement is for products or
serwices offered on the premises where the sign is located. Mr. Maldonado's atternpts to
use this billboard without a Caltrans "off-premises" lrrmit led to a state court nuisance
action that resulted in a permane,lrt injunction against Mr. Maldonado.a
Following the state court's injunction, Mr. Maldonado continud to test the
restrictions in the COAA, and has twice been found in contempt of the court's injunction.
Shortly before filing his constitutional claim in lideral court Mr. Maldonado posted
messages on his billboard that he described as'foliticaVreligous," such as *In God We
Trust," *We Pray for World Peacg" and "Help Stop Terrorisrm." He also posted a sign
from the nonprofit group Habitat for Humanity.
The ensuing federal lawsuit alleged that the COAA violates theFirst Amendment
on its face, and as applied to Mr. Maldonado and his various advertiserre,nts. After the
trial court dismissed his claims on procedural grounds, Mr. Maldonado appealed to the
Ninth Circuit which, on June 4,z0M,reversod the lower court's dismissal and directed
that the claims be tried on their merits. You have not advised us that this trial has been
completed, or that Mr. Maldonado has obtained an off-prernises permit from Calkans.
3 Maldonado v. Harris,370 F.3d 945 (9s Ctr.2AU). Orn accormt of the pertinert facts is dtrivcd fiom the
information you provided to us in your two requests for advice, augmemted by details given in the recently-
published Ninth Circuit decision, and from the earlier decision ofthe California Court of Appeal iaPeople
ex rel. Department of Transportationv. Mqldomdo,86Cal. App.4* 1225 (2W)-
o The Califomia Court of Appeal (supra,86 Cal. App. 4* at 1228-1229) explained that Mr. Maldonado
attempted to circumvent the COAA ttrough the lease of unused office space on the premises while each
"lessor's" advertisement appeared on the billboard. These advertisers included the Stanford Shopping
Center, the Stanford University Athletics Deparhnent, the Palo Alto Holiday Inn, Skyway Cellular, and
Golden Time Jewelers.


FileNo. l-04-176
Page No. 3
In your original request for advice on June 22,ZAM, you told us that because the
state court injunction balred Mr. Maldonado from placing "commetrsial" advertisements
on his billboard, he was hoping to rent the billboard for "noncommercial" (defined as not
pertaining to a good or service) political advertising. Mr. Maldonado's atternpt was
based on a notice he sent out to numerous federal, state and local candidates, including
representatives ofboth majorpolitical parties, solicitingbids of $10,000 ormoreper
month, which he characterized as a reasonable rate in light of a claimed $30,000 to
$40,000 monthly charge to "commercial" advertisers for comparable billbomd space.
Your fundamental question was whether the lease of this space at less than the fair
market value for "commercial" advertisers would be classified as an in-kind contribution
to a candidate who would pay $10,000 per month - far less than the "comm€trsial" rate.
You observed that the fair market value for your client's billboard was easily established
when the market was "commercial" advertisers, but you expressed rmcertainty at how -
apart from an "auction" process - your client might establish the fair market value of a
billboard reskicted to "noncommercial" advertisers due to a legal impairment. You
offered your opinion that the fair market value under the circrmrstances was '\rhatever
someone was willinglopay," but indicated that "my client wants an official opinion."
Our letter in reqronse said: "We cannot act as the finder of fact. We cannot advise you
that your client's willingness to engage in this hansaction establishes the fairmartet
value for the billboard." (Zell Adice LetterNo. A-M-142.)
On the following day you submitted a request that we reconsider this position,
noting first that the effect of our letter was to abridge your client's freedom ofpress, and
then that you had not sought a factual determination of fair market value, but were
seeking a legal conclusion based on assumed facts. On this latterpoint, you reiterated that
'the billboard space is generally available to anyone at $1O000 per side, per month." In
a subsequent telephone conversation, I explained the process by which persons seeking
advice from the Commission could request reconsideration of prior advicq and I agreed
to address your disagreernent with our earlier advicg since you told me that your client
will considerre,nting billbomd space for campaign advertisements in the future.
ANALYSIS
In our prior letter, we cited longstanding Commission advice that *[{lair mmket
value is whatever it would cost the candidate or committee to obtain the same or similar
goods or services on the open market." Because your client effectively conducted an
auction among political candidates who might haye use for an advertisement on his
billboard, you af,gue that the price o{fered as a result of this auction establishes a fair
mmket value for campaign advertisements displayod on billboards like the one ovmd by
Mr. Maldonado. But you describe a "market" that is reshicted to just one subset of the
largermarket of billboard advertisers, a limitation on marketability that you athibute to
the permanent injunction. In fact, it appears that the sweep of the injrmction exte,nds to
all"off-prunises" advertisements, including those you characterize as'honcommercial"
political advertising. Our unwillingness to comment on your client's proposed transaction


File No. l-04-176
Page No. 4
in our prior letter was due to the uncertainties of law and fact generated by your client's
peculiar circumstances.
In reconsidering our initial unwillingness to agtee that a $10,000 bid established
the fairmarket value of your client's billboard, \ile maynot overlook the fundamental
problem underlying any assumption that an auction among candidates might establish the
fair market value of this billboard. Business & Professions Code section 5350 is the core
provision of the COAA relevant to Mr. Maldonado's difficulties, and it provides that no
person shall place any adv€rtising display within 660 feet of a landscaped highway
without first having secured a Caltrans permit. Section 5272 proides exceptions
generally applicable to advertisements relating to the olyner or occupant of the premises
where the billboard is located. As the Califomia appellate court observed, t}re trial court
enjoined Mr. Maldonado "from displalng any advertising on his billboard without
perrnission from Caltrans and generally enjoined him from displaying any on-premises
advertising unless it was for business actually conducted on the pretrnises." (Maldonado,
supra,86 Cal. App. 4* at 1230.) The injunction mandates compliance with ihe COAA,
and particularly with sections 5330 and 5272. As long as these statutes and the injunction
remain undisturbed, and Mr. Maldonado does not secure a Caltrans permit, there is no
Iawful 'tnarket" for off-premises advertising on this billboard.
We understand that the point of Mr. Maldonado's ongoing Iederal court action is
to probe the injunction, and underlying law, for First Amendment exceptions that might
ove,lride Caltrans' power to regulate the time, place and manner of Mr- Maldonado's
advertisements. But we cannot comply with yorr request for advice on a legal question
that assumes a"fair market value" for advertising space that cannot lawfully be marketed.
We will be happy to address concrete questions regarding the sale of oflprerrises
advertising space to candidates if and when Mr. Maldonado obtains relief from the Ninth
Circuit, and/orapermit from Caltrans that e,nables him to lawfullymarket this space to
such advertisers. As matters now stand, there is no basis for revising our prior advice.
Thereforg we do not alter the conclusions we reached earlier in the Zell Adice Letter,
No. 4-04-142.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
LuisaMe,nchaca
General Cormsel!*r.il"-/U
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Cormsel, Legal Division
LTwjg

an assumed fair market value of $10,000 per side per month.
